q: The Cabinet Mission Plan was proposed in which year?
explanation: The Cabinet Mission Plan was proposed in March 1946. It was sent by the British government (led by Lord Pethick-Lawrence, Sir Stafford Cripps, and A.V. Alexander) to negotiate the transfer of power. It proposed a united India with a weak centre, provincial groupings, and a constituent assembly. The INC initially accepted it but later disagreed over interpretations. The Muslim League accepted it, then withdrew acceptance in July 1946.

q: Who was the last Viceroy of British India and the first Governor-General of independent India?
explanation: Lord Mountbatten (Viceroy March-August 1947; Governor-General 1947-1948) was the last Viceroy of British India and the first Governor-General of independent India (for India, not Pakistan — Jinnah became Pakistan's first Governor-General). C. Rajagopalachari was the first Indian Governor-General (1948-1950). Lord Wavell (1943-1947) preceded Mountbatten as Viceroy.

q: The Mountbatten Plan (June 3 Plan) was announced on which date?
explanation: The Mountbatten Plan (June 3 Plan) was announced on June 3, 1947. It proposed the partition of India into two dominions — India and Pakistan — and set the date for transfer of power as August 15, 1947 (advanced from June 1948). The plan was accepted by both the INC and the Muslim League. The Indian Independence Act 1947 was based on this plan.

q: The Indian Independence Act 1947 was passed by the British Parliament on which date?
explanation: The Indian Independence Act 1947 was passed by the British Parliament on July 18, 1947 (received royal assent on July 18). It came into effect on August 15, 1947 — the date of independence. The Act was based on the Mountbatten Plan (June 3 Plan) and created two independent dominions: India and Pakistan.

q: India became independent on which date?
explanation: India became independent on August 15, 1947. Pakistan became independent on August 14, 1947 (one day earlier, so Mountbatten could attend both ceremonies). January 26, 1950 was the date the Indian Constitution came into force (Republic Day). Nehru gave his 'Tryst with Destiny' speech at midnight on August 14-15, 1947.

q: Who gave the famous 'Tryst with Destiny' speech at midnight on August 14-15, 1947?
explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru gave the 'Tryst with Destiny' speech at the Constituent Assembly at midnight on August 14-15, 1947. The opening words: 'Long years ago we made a tryst with destiny, and now the time comes when we shall redeem our pledge, not wholly or in full measure, but very substantially.' Nehru became the first Prime Minister of independent India.

q: Who was the first Governor-General of Pakistan?
explanation: M.A. Jinnah became the first Governor-General of Pakistan (August 14, 1947 - September 11, 1948). He was the leader of the Muslim League and is known as 'Quaid-e-Azam' (Great Leader) in Pakistan. Liaquat Ali Khan was the first Prime Minister of Pakistan. Lord Mountbatten was the first Governor-General of India (not Pakistan).

q: Who was the first Indian Governor-General of independent India?
explanation: C. Rajagopalachari (Rajaji) was the first Indian Governor-General of independent India (1948-1950). Lord Mountbatten was the first Governor-General (1947-1948) but was British. After Rajaji, the office was replaced by the President of India (Rajendra Prasad, 1950). Nehru was the first PM. Rajendra Prasad was the first President.

q: The Radcliffe Line that partitioned India and Pakistan was drawn by whom?
explanation: The Radcliffe Line (the boundary between India and Pakistan) was drawn by Sir Cyril Radcliffe, a British lawyer who had never been to India before. He was given just 5 weeks to demarcate the boundary between India and Pakistan in Bengal and Punjab. The boundary was announced on August 17, 1947 (two days AFTER independence) — causing massive confusion and communal violence during partition.

q: Who raised the cry 'Divide and Quit' during India's independence movement?
explanation: M.A. Jinnah raised the cry 'Divide and Quit' — a play on the 'Quit India' slogan. Jinnah demanded the partition of India and the creation of Pakistan. The Lahore Resolution (1940, also called the Pakistan Resolution) demanded separate Muslim states. The 71st BPSC asked this question — the answer is (A) M.A. Jinnah.

q: The Lahore Resolution (1940), also known as the Pakistan Resolution, was passed by which party?
explanation: The Lahore Resolution (March 23, 1940) was passed by the Muslim League at its Lahore session, presided by Jinnah. It demanded separate Muslim states in the Muslim-majority areas of India. March 23 is celebrated as 'Pakistan Day.' The resolution was drafted by Sikandar Hayat Khan (Premier of Punjab) and moved by A.K. Fazlul Haq (Premier of Bengal).

q: The Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) proposed which key feature?
options: A united India with a weak centre and provincial groupings
Immediate partition into India and Pakistan
A federal system with a strong centre
explanation: The Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) proposed a united India with: (1) a weak centre (only defence, foreign affairs, communications); (2) provincial groupings (Group A: Hindu-majority provinces, Group B: Muslim-majority provinces in the northwest, Group C: Bengal and Assam); (3) a constituent assembly to frame the constitution; (4) an interim government. The INC accepted it initially; the Muslim League accepted then withdrew (July 1946). The plan ultimately failed, leading to partition.

q: Arrange in chronological order: 1. Cabinet Mission Plan 2. Mountbatten Plan 3. Indian Independence Act 4. Lahore Resolution
explanation: Lahore Resolution (March 1940) → Cabinet Mission Plan (March 1946) → Mountbatten Plan (June 3, 1947) → Indian Independence Act (July 18, 1947). The Lahore Resolution demanded Pakistan; the Cabinet Mission tried to keep India united; the Mountbatten Plan opted for partition; the Independence Act implemented it.

q: Who was the first Home Minister of independent India, responsible for the integration of princely states?
explanation: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel was the first Deputy Prime Minister and Home Minister of independent India (1947-1950). He was responsible for the integration of 562 princely states into the Indian Union — using a combination of diplomacy, persuasion, and military action. He is known as the 'Iron Man of India.' The integration was one of the greatest administrative achievements in modern Indian history. Patel was assisted by V.P. Menon (Secretary of the States Ministry).

q: Which princely state was integrated into India through a military action (police action) in 1948?
explanation: Hyderabad was integrated into India through a military action (called 'Operation Polo' or 'Police Action') in September 1948. The Nizam of Hyderabad, Osman Ali Khan, wanted to remain independent or join Pakistan. After negotiations failed and the Nizam's Razakars (a militia) unleashed violence, the Indian Army launched Operation Polo (September 13-18, 1948) and annexed Hyderabad. Junagadh was integrated through a plebiscite (1948). Kashmir's accession is disputed.

q: The accession of Jammu and Kashmir to India took place in which year and under what instrument?
options: 1947, Instrument of Accession signed by Maharaja Hari Singh
explanation: Jammu and Kashmir acceded to India on October 26, 1947, through the Instrument of Accession signed by Maharaja Hari Singh (the Hindu ruler of the Muslim-majority state). Pakistan had sent tribal raiders (October 1947) to seize Kashmir; the Maharaja sought India's help; India agreed only after the accession was signed. This led to the first India-Pakistan war (1947-48). The issue was taken to the UN (1948). Article 370 (special status) was added in 1949.

q: The Constituent Assembly of India was formed under which plan?
explanation: The Constituent Assembly of India was formed under the Cabinet Mission Plan (1946). Elections to the Constituent Assembly were held in July 1946 (indirect elections by provincial legislatures). The first meeting was held on December 9, 1946. Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha was the first temporary president; Dr. Rajendra Prasad was elected permanent president on December 11, 1946. The Assembly drafted the Indian Constitution (1946-1949), which came into force on January 26, 1950.

q: Who was the first President of the Constituent Assembly?
explanation: Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha was the first (temporary) President of the Constituent Assembly (December 9, 1946). Dr. Rajendra Prasad was elected as the permanent President on December 11, 1946. The Constituent Assembly had 389 members (296 from British India + 93 from princely states). Note: this is a tricky question — 'first President' can refer to either the temporary first president (Sinha) or the first permanent/elected president (Prasad). BPSC typically asks about Dr. Rajendra Prasad as the President.

q: Who presided over the first session of the Constituent Assembly?
explanation: Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha presided over the first session of the Constituent Assembly (December 9, 1946) as the temporary president. He was a Congress leader from Bihar. On December 11, 1946, Dr. Rajendra Prasad was elected as the permanent President. This is a Bihar-specific question — Sachchidananda Sinha was from Bihar (Arrah).

q: Who was the first President of independent India?
explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the first President of independent India (January 26, 1950 - May 13, 1962). He was elected by the Constituent Assembly on January 24, 1950. He served two terms (1950-1957, 1957-1962) and is the only President to have been elected for two terms. Before becoming President, he was the President of the Constituent Assembly (1946-1949). He was from Bihar (Siwan district).

q: The Indian Constitution came into force on which date?
explanation: The Indian Constitution came into force on January 26, 1950 (Republic Day). It was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on November 26, 1949 (Constitution Day). January 26 was chosen because the INC had declared 'Purna Swaraj' (complete independence) on January 26, 1930 (Lahore session). The Constitution replaced the Government of India Act 1935 as the constitutional framework of India.

q: Who was the first Prime Minister of independent India?
explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru was the first Prime Minister of independent India (August 15, 1947 - May 27, 1964). He served for 17 years until his death. He gave the 'Tryst with Destiny' speech at midnight on August 14-15, 1947. Sardar Patel was the Deputy PM and Home Minister. Nehru is considered the architect of modern India — he shaped India's foreign policy (Non-Aligned Movement) and economic planning (Five-Year Plans).

q: Bihar's connection with independence and partition was through:
options: Dr. Rajendra Prasad as first President of the Constituent Assembly (and later President of India)
The partition of Bihar into Bihar and Jharkhand
explanation: Bihar's connection with independence was through Dr. Rajendra Prasad — the first President of the Constituent Assembly (1946) and the first President of India (1950). He was from Bihar (Siwan district). Also, Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha (from Arrah, Bihar) was the first temporary president of the Constituent Assembly. Sri Krishna Singh became the first Chief Minister of Bihar (1946-1961). Bihar remained part of India during partition (not affected by the Radcliffe Line).

q: Subhash Chandra Bose resigned from the presidency of the INC in 1939. Who succeeded him?
options: More than one of the above
explanation: The 69th/70th/71st BPSC asked this question. Subhash Chandra Bose resigned as INC President in April 1939 (after disagreements with Gandhi). Dr. Rajendra Prasad was appointed as the next INC President (1939). The BPSC answer was (D) 'More than one of the above' due to the question's framing. After resigning, Bose formed the Forward Bloc (1939).

q: Who was the last Viceroy of India before Mountbatten?
explanation: Lord Wavell (Viceroy 1943-1947) was the last Viceroy before Mountbatten. He attempted the Wavell Plan (1945) and the Simla Conference (June-July 1945) — both failed. He was replaced by Mountbatten in March 1947. Lord Linlithgow (1936-1943) was Viceroy during WWII and Quit India. Lord Reading (1921-1926) was Viceroy during the Non-Cooperation Movement. Lord Irwin (1926-1931) was Viceroy during the Civil Disobedience Movement.
